subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clearly stated and operationalised directional and null hypotheses
Aim is explicitly linked to relevance in student learning and prior findings
Theory/model (Transactive Memory Theory) is accurately described and connected to the study
Relevance to âGoogle effectâ is well explained in context
weaknesses: Minor imprecision in null hypothesis wording
Misstatement of source details (publication year error)
Brief discussion of model could be expanded for depth
Empirical support (citations) for certain claims is limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the 5â6 descriptors with clear aim, theory linkage, and operationalisation of variables. Minor issuesâslightly imprecise hypothesis phrasing and a citation errorâprevent a top-band 6 award, so a score of 5 reflects strong but not flawless performance.

strengths: Design choice is not only described but justified by minimising order effects
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are clearly defined and linked to validity
Multiple environmental controls and ethical considerations are robustly identified
Rationale for control measures (e.g., unfamiliar facts, signage) demonstrates attention to reliability
weaknesses: Discussion of randomisation method is missing
Convenience sampling generalisability concerns are not fully addressed
Link between materials and specific confounding controls needs elaboration
Pilot study or parental consent procedures are not discussed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section meets all topâband criteria, with both design and sampling explained and clear control measures. Minor gaps in methodological detail (randomisation, generalisability) prevent perfection, so a full 4/4 reflects very strong work.

strengths: Inferential (MannâWhitney U) and descriptive statistics are appropriately selected
Findings are interpreted in direct relation to the hypothesis and prior research
Graphical method correctly addresses the hypothesis with error bars
Interpretation logically ties statistical outcome to the null hypothesis
weaknesses: Probable calculation/reporting errors and omitted effect size
Graph lacks full labelling (axes titles, units, sample sizes)
Interpretation section is concise but could further analyse trends and error bar implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical choices and interpretation link clearly to hypotheses, fulfilling topâband intent. However, accuracy issues (calculations, missing effect size), labelling errors, and incomplete data presentation limit this to 4/6 rather than a higher mark.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are stated, explained, and clearly tied to validity and reliability
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations with sound justification
Limitations discussion engages with multiple relevant aspects of the study
Proposed modifications target key methodological issues
weaknesses: Findings discussion lacks deeper theoretical integration with Transactive Memory Theory
Some limitation points overlap and could be streamlined
Specificity in modification proposals (e.g., power analysis thresholds) is limited
Broader implications of nonâsignificant findings are not fully explored
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation shows clear understanding of strengths, limitations, and justified improvements in line with topâband descriptors. The relatively brief theoretical discussion and minor overlap in points constrain the score to 4/6 rather than a perfect 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph with error bars is provided and directly relates to the hypothesis (Delete vs Save mean recall).  Some labelling/detail (axes titles, units) is limited and the sample sizes are not marked, indicating minor errors.  Thus the 3-4 band is reached but not the top mark.

The student presents the statistical outcome (U = 46.5, p =.85) and explicitly interprets this in relation to the null hypothesis (âcannot rejectâ) and to Sparrowâs original findings.  Interpretation is logical and grounded in the obtained data.  While concise, it fully meets the criteria for linking findings and hypothesis, meriting a 5 in the top band.

A directional (research) hypothesis and an operationalised null hypothesis are provided.  The IV is clearly the instruction condition (Delete vs Save) and the DV is the number of facts correctly recalled, both measurable.  While wording is not perfectly concise, the variables are operationalised, satisfying the requirements for the top band; a mid-band 5 is assigned because phrasing of the null (âwouldnât recall considerably moreâ) is slightly imprecise.

Convenience sampling is identified and the characteristics/criteria (IB1 students over 16, unfamiliar with Sparrow) are presented.  The consequences for external validity are briefly explained, demonstrating reasoning rather than mere listing; hence the explanatory 3-4 band is met at the upper level.

Transactive Memory Theory is described and explicitly connected to the reliance on Google.  Sparrow et al.âs study is summarised and linked to the present investigation, showing the theoretical basis for the replication.  Description is accurate but somewhat brief; nevertheless, the link between theory and the current study is made explicit, meeting the descriptor for the 5-6 band.

Findings are related to Sparrow et al. and to the broader Google-effect concept, but the discussion is brief and lacks depth about theoretical implications.  This constitutes description with reference, matching the 3-4 band, and a lower mark of 3 is appropriate.

The student clearly states the aim â to investigate whether anticipation of future access affects recall in high-school students â and explicitly situates this within the broader issue of the âGoogle effect/digital amnesia.â  In two sentences the relevance of this aim to everyday student learning and to Sparrow et al.âs earlier findings is explained, demonstrating more than a mere identification.  The explanation is adequate but not deeply analytical, so the lower end of the 5-6 band is appropriate.

Multiple strengths and limitations (sample size, participant allocation, design issues, pronunciation, definition of âcorrectâ) are identified and each is explained for its potential impact on validity or reliability.  The relevance to the investigation is explicit throughout, fulfilling the explanatory requirement of the 5-6 band at a mid-level score.

Several controls (standardised instructions, signage, fact selection) and materials are listed and, in places, the rationale is stated (e.g., signs to minimise distraction, unfamiliar facts to reduce prior knowledge).  The explanation is uneven and occasionally slips into description, so a mid-band score of 3 is most fitting.

At least three concrete modifications (increase sample/time, text-to-speech, language proficiency checks) are directly tied to stated limitations and are justified in terms of improving reliability and validity.  The linkage and justification satisfy the top-band descriptor; a score of 5 is assigned as the justifications, while clear, are not exceptionally detailed.

The student not only outlines that an independent-measures design was chosen but also justifies this by reference to minimising order effects and fatigue.  This moves beyond description into explanation, aligning with the 3-4 band.  Rationale is succinct but clear, meriting the top of that band.

Appropriate descriptive statistics and an inferential Mann-Whitney U test are selected with justification for non-parametric choice.  However, there are probable calculation or reporting errors (e.g., median higher than mean despite skew, small sample critical value misquoted), so accuracy is imperfect.  This matches the 3-4 descriptor but warrants the lower mark in that band.

annotations.comment: The student adeptly introduces the Google Effect and connects to Transactive Memory Theory, but the reference to Carrâs article misstates the original publication year. Verify and correct source details to enhance academic accuracy.

Description of Sparrow et al.âs study is concise but lacks explanation of how participant characteristics in the original and current samples differ. Elaborate on context and familiarity to strengthen theoretical linkage.
The null hypothesis wording (âwouldnât recall considerably moreâ) is vague. Rephrase to specify exact expected difference or direction (e.g., âno difference in mean recall between groupsâ).
Sampling criteria are clearly defined (age, familiarity), demonstrating ethical rigour. This strengthens validity by controlling for participant bias.
Convenience sampling introduces external validity concerns. Consider alternative techniques (e.g., stratified or random sampling) or discuss implications for generalisability.
Rationale for using an independent-measures design is clear, but referencing Sparrowâs original 2Ã2 matrix may confuse readers. Clarify how the two conditions map onto the original factors.
Procedure description lacks detail on the randomisation method used to allocate participants. Specify how random assignment was achieved to improve methodological clarity.
Materials table lists items clearly but omits explanation of how each supports control of confounding variables; link each item to validity or reliability functions.
Multiple environmental controls are well identified (standard instructions, signage, silence), demonstrating attention to reliability.
Facts were controlled for length and familiarity, but the choice of eight words lacks justification. Cite literature on working memory constraints to support this operationalisation.
Reporting of U value is correct, but the exact p-value and an effect size (e.g., r) should be included for fuller inferential interpretation.
Discussion restates results but lacks theoretical integration; link the non-significant finding to Transactive Memory Theory to deepen evaluation.
Proposed language-proficiency checks are pertinent, but specify which assessment and threshold would ensure linguistic comparability.
Ethical considerations are comprehensive, including consent, anonymity, and debriefing, which strengthens the studyâs ethical rigour.
Interpretation that high SD implies non-normality is imprecise; include a normality test (e.g., Shapiro-Wilk) to justify the use of non-parametric inference.
Descriptive statistics table omits sample sizes; include n for each group to contextualise mean, median and SD values.
Graph lacks axis labels, units, and annotations of sample size. Improve labelling and include error bar definitions to clarify data presentation.
Strengths section cogently identifies sampling and power limitations, explicitly linking to validity concerns, which enhances credibility.
Limitations discussion is thorough but overlaps points on randomisation twice. Streamline by focusing on the most critical biases.
Modifications are directly tied to stated limitations and justified in terms of reliability and validity improvements.
Increasing sample size is crucial; consider conducting an a priori power analysis to determine the optimal number of participants.
Conclusion restates results but misses discussion of broader educational implications or future research questions; enrich with forward-looking insights.
Was a pilot study conducted to test instruction clarity and timing? Including pilot data or feedback would strengthen reliability and procedural validity.
Consider whether parental consent was required for participants under 18; clarify compliance with adolescent research ethics.
Reliance on Carrâs argument that external sources limit skill development is asserted without empirical support; integrate peer-reviewed citations to substantiate this claim.
Graph description is truncated, leaving interpretation incomplete. Ensure continuity and fully analyse trends and error bar implications.
